# Appendix E: Context Health Checklist

This appendix provides a practical checklist for assessing the health of a context engineering system. Use it during periodic reviews (weekly or per-sprint) and at quality gates before major releases.

## Structural Health

These checks assess whether the foundational context artefacts are complete and consistent.

### Domain Model

- [ ] Domain model exists and is versioned
- [ ] All key domain terms are defined with unambiguous definitions
- [ ] Synonym groups are documented (term X = term Y in this context)
- [ ] Relationships between concepts are explicitly typed (not just "related to")
- [ ] Domain model has been reviewed by a domain expert (not just the engineer who wrote it)
- [ ] No orphan terms (terms defined but not used in any artefact)
- [ ] No undefined terms (terms used in artefacts but not in the domain model)

### Knowledge Graph

- [ ] Knowledge graph is populated and queryable
- [ ] Term coverage: >80% of domain model terms appear in the KG
- [ ] Synonym coverage: key terms have at least 2-3 synonyms defined
- [ ] Relationship coverage: key entity pairs have typed relationships
- [ ] Role-based graphs are configured for each active context role
- [ ] KG build time is acceptable (<1s for graphs under 100K terms)
- [ ] Search results are deterministic (same query returns same results)

### Vocabulary

- [ ] Vocabulary file exists and is maintained
- [ ] Industry-standard terms are included (not just internal jargon)
- [ ] Acronyms are expanded in the vocabulary
- [ ] Vocabulary is validated against actual usage in codebase/documents

## Operational Health

These checks assess whether the context system is functioning correctly in production.

### Retrieval Quality

- [ ] Retrieval accuracy is measured (% of queries returning relevant results)
- [ ] Precision at top-5 results is tracked
- [ ] Zero-result queries are logged and investigated
- [ ] Retrieval latency is within acceptable bounds (<100ms for deterministic, <2s for semantic)

### Context Freshness

- [ ] Knowledge graph was updated within the last sprint/iteration
- [ ] No stale entries (documents referenced but no longer existing)
- [ ] New code/documents added since last KG update are identified
- [ ] Temporal metadata (last_updated, source_date) is present on KG entries

### Drift Detection

- [ ] Baseline context snapshot exists
- [ ] Drift is measured at regular intervals
- [ ] Vocabulary drift: new terms appearing in usage that are not in the KG
- [ ] Assumption drift: conventions established in recent sessions that contradict earlier ones
- [ ] Relationship drift: entity connections that have changed meaning
- [ ] Drift threshold triggers are defined (e.g., >15% = warning, >30% = critical)

## Governance Health

These checks assess whether the context management process is sound.

### Change Management

- [ ] Context changes are tracked (who changed what, when, why)
- [ ] Impact analysis is performed before significant changes
- [ ] Changes to the domain model require review/approval
- [ ] Vocabulary additions follow a defined process (not ad-hoc)

### Quality Gates

- [ ] Pre-LLM validation is automated (syntax, semantics, grounding)
- [ ] Post-LLM validation checks outputs against KG
- [ ] Gate pass rates are tracked over time
- [ ] Failed gates produce actionable feedback (not just "FAILED")

### Documentation

- [ ] Context architecture is documented (which systems, how they connect)
- [ ] KG schema is documented (node types, relationship types, properties)
- [ ] Role definitions are documented (who uses which vocabulary)
- [ ] Runbook exists for common context operations (rebuild KG, add terms, fix drift)

## Agent-Specific Health

These checks apply when AI agents are consuming the context.

### Agent Context Configuration

- [ ] Each agent role has a defined context scope (what it can see)
- [ ] System prompts reference the knowledge graph for grounding
- [ ] CLAUDE.md (or equivalent) is maintained and current
- [ ] Skill definitions include context requirements
- [ ] Token budget is defined and monitored per agent

### Multi-Agent Coherence

- [ ] Shared vocabulary is consistent across agents
- [ ] Handover documents are generated between agent sessions
- [ ] No contradictory conventions established by different agents
- [ ] Agent outputs are validated against the shared domain model

### Feedback Loop

- [ ] Failed agent actions are captured for learning
- [ ] Agent queries with zero results are logged
- [ ] Context gaps discovered by agents are fed back to the KG
- [ ] Agent performance metrics are tracked over time

## Scoring Guide

For each section, count the number of checked items and calculate a percentage:

| Score | Status | Action |
|-------|--------|--------|
| 90-100% | Healthy | Continue monitoring |
| 70-89% | Adequate | Address gaps in next sprint |
| 50-69% | At Risk | Prioritise context improvements |
| Below 50% | Critical | Stop feature work, fix context foundations |

## Recommended Review Cadence

| Check Category | Frequency | Typical Duration |
|---------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Retrieval quality spot-check | Daily (automated) | 0 min (CI/CD) |
| Drift detection | Weekly (automated) | 0 min (scheduled) |
| Full structural health | Per sprint | 30-60 min |
| Full governance review | Monthly | 1-2 hours |
| Agent coherence audit | Per major release | 2-4 hours |

## Key Sources

- Chapter 14: Validation and Quality Gates (KLS protocol, drift detection)
- Chapter 18: Enterprise Context Governance (gated lifecycle, health metrics)
- Chapter 7: Terraphim Graph Embeddings (deterministic search properties)
- Chapter 15: Multi-Agent Context Management (coherence patterns)
